1: The verb "went up" (wayyaʿal) is the directional standard for travel from Egypt toward Canaan — topographically accurate (the Sinai peninsula rises into the hill country of Canaan) and covenantally resonant (return to the land of promise). The mention of Lot's continued presence with Abram reinstates the covenant household unit that the Egypt episode of Genesis 12 had left intact: "his wife and everything he had, and Lot went with him." The Negev functions as the transitional zone between Egypt and Canaan in the patriarchal itineraries — Abram re-enters his covenant territory by the same route he left it.
2: "Abram had become very wealthy in livestock and in silver and gold" — the three-category wealth inventory (livestock, silver/monetary metal, gold/precious metal) represents the primary forms of ANE pastoral wealth. Livestock was productive capital (herds grew and generated income); silver was the standard monetary medium in Mesopotamia and Canaan (coined money had not yet been invented — silver was weighed by the shekel); gold was the prestige metal of the wealthy. This wealth notation fulfills the Egypt episode's dark irony: Abram's deception (Genesis 12:11-13) was followed by divine protection and material gain. The narrator notes the wealth without moralizing its source.
3: "From the Negev he went from place to place until he came to Bethel" — the phrase "from place to place" (lĕmasSāʿāyw, literally "by his stages/encampments") describes the semi-nomadic pattern of moving between established camping stations. Abram is retracing his original northward journey from 12:9 in reverse. The journey to Bethel is a covenant-recommitment movement: returning to the altar he built on his first entry into Canaan (Genesis 12:8) before the Egypt detour interrupted the covenant journey. The geographic return corresponds to a spiritual return.
4: "Where he had first built an altar. There Abram called on the name of the LORD" — the resumption of covenant worship at the exact site of the prior altar establishes covenant continuity after the Egypt episode. The phrase "called on the name of the LORD" (wayyiqrāʾ bĕšēm YHWH) is the standard covenantal worship formula established in Genesis 4:26 (Seth's line) and enacted by Abram at each altar site (Genesis 12:8; Genesis 13:4; Genesis 21:33; Genesis 26:25). Returning to the Bethel altar rather than building a new one signals that the prior covenant-worship stands — Abram is not starting over but resuming.
5: "Lot, who was moving about with Abram, also had flocks and herds and tents" — Lot's wealth is presented as a consequence of his association with Abram's covenant-household. The blessing that flows to "all peoples" through Abram (Genesis 12:3) begins with his nearest traveling companion. Lot's prosperity through covenant-proximity is not elaborated theologically but embedded narratively: the one who stays near the covenant-bearer shares in the overflow. The listing of "flocks and herds and tents" mirrors Abram's own wealth-description in v2, signaling that the two households have become comparably sized.
6: "The land could not support them while they stayed together, for their possessions were so great that they were not able to stay together" — pastoral economics in the ANE required large land areas per household. Ancient Near Eastern pastoral law codes (reflected in documents from Mari and Nuzi) dealt extensively with grazing rights, water access, and inter-household pastoral conflicts. The Canaanite landscape — hilly, with limited valleys suitable for extensive grazing — had a finite carrying capacity. The conflict is not moral failure but material reality: too many animals for too little land. The land itself imposes the separation.
7: "Quarreling arose between Abram's herders and Lot's herders. The Canaanites and Perizzites were also living in the land at that time" — the mention of existing inhabitants adds contextual pressure: the available pastureland was not exclusively available to Abram and Lot. The Perizzites (Pĕrizzî) — "those of the open country" or "village-dwellers" (from pĕrāzāh, an unwalled town) — are listed among the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Canaan throughout the OT (Deuteronomy 7:1; Joshua 3:10; Judges 1:4-5; Ezra 9:1; Nehemiah 9:8). Their presence signals that the pastureland conflict occurs within a contested landscape where the covenant-household must negotiate space with existing residents.
8: "For we are close relatives (ʾănāšîm ʾăḥîm)" — literally "we are men who are brothers." The appeal to kinship solidarity is the ANE basis for peaceful resolution of internal disputes. Family honor in the ancient world required that disputes within the kinship group be resolved without bloodshed — inter-family violence was a matter of clan-shame. Abram's appeal ("let's not have quarreling between you and me") is both a social norm invocation and a personal request: the covenant-patriarch is protecting the household's internal cohesion. His generosity in v9 — offering Lot first choice — goes beyond the requirement of the social norm.
9: "Is not the whole land before you? Let's part company" — Abram's offer of first choice to Lot is remarkable from an ANE honor perspective. As the elder kinsman, the covenant-bearer, and the one who initiated the journey, Abram had both the social right and the expectation of choosing first. His deferral to Lot inverts the hierarchical priority that ANE culture would have expected. The covenant context explains the inversion: Abram can afford to be generous because YHWH has already promised him "all the land" (vv14-17). Covenant-security generates covenant-generosity — trust in divine provision liberates from the competition for advantage.
10: "Lot looked around and saw that the whole plain of the Jordan toward Zoar was well watered, like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt" — the two comparisons frame the Jordan valley's appeal through Lot's knowledge: the "garden of the LORD" (Eden-like, from Genesis 2:8-10's river-watered garden) and Egypt (the Nile-irrigated land Lot had just experienced, Genesis 12:10-20). The Jordan valley in the Bronze Age was indeed heavily forested and irrigated — the Dead Sea region was not always the barren salt-flat it became after Sodom's destruction. The narrator's parenthetical ("before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah") is a pointed foreshadowing: the choice that looks best will lead to catastrophe.
11: "Lot chose for himself the whole plain of the Jordan and set out toward the east" — the eastward movement carries the narrative's consistent theological direction: Adam and Eve were expelled east of Eden (Genesis 3:24); Cain went east of Eden to Nod (Genesis 4:16); the Babel builders moved eastward (Genesis 11:2). Now Lot moves eastward toward the Jordan plain, away from Canaan (the covenant land, to the west). The directional choice maps the covenant trajectories: Abram stays in Canaan (covenant land), Lot moves east (away from the covenant center). "The two men parted company" — the separation is clean and mutual, without bitterness, but permanent in its covenant significance.
12: "Abram lived in the land of Canaan, while Lot lived among the cities of the plain and pitched his tents near Sodom" — the geographic contrast encodes the theological contrast. The land of Canaan is the covenant-territory; the cities of the plain are identified with Sodom, already under divine condemnation (v13). Lot's progressive entanglement with Sodom — from "near Sodom" here (Genesis 13:12) to "sitting in the gateway of Sodom" (Genesis 19:1, a position of civic leadership and commerce) — charts a gradual cultural assimilation into the condemned city. The tents near Sodom mark only the beginning of a trajectory that ends in the rescue of Genesis 19.
13: "The people of Sodom were wicked (rāʿîm) and were sinning greatly (ḥaṭṭāʾîm mĕʾōḏ) against the LORD" — the narrator's judicial verdict follows immediately after Lot's choice to settle near Sodom, creating a stark dramatic irony: Lot chose the best land without knowing its moral condition before YHWH. The double intensification (wicked + sinning greatly) functions as an anticipatory verdict — the reader knows what Lot does not, and the narrator is preparing for the destruction of Genesis 19. "Against the LORD" (laYHWH) specifies that the Sodomites' wickedness is not merely social disruption but covenant-violation against the divine sovereign of all nations.
14: "The LORD said to Abram after Lot had parted from him" — the covenant reaffirmation comes precisely after the separation: Lot's departure does not diminish the promise; YHWH reaffirms and expands it. The timing is significant: the direction given to Abram — "look north and south, east and west" — is the four-direction survey formula for possessing territory. In ANE legal practice, the symbolic survey of territory from an elevated viewpoint was a form of territorial claim-staking. The same gesture will be enacted on Mount Nebo (Deuteronomy 34:1-4) before the conquest — and what Abram sees promised, Moses will see about to be fulfilled.
15: "All the land that you see I will give to you and your offspring (lĕzarʿăkā) forever (ʿad-ʿôlām)" — the promise includes the temporal qualifier "forever" (ʿad-ʿôlām) — the same ʿôlām (everlasting) language of the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:16). The land promise is not provisional but permanent. The word "offspring" (zeraʿ) is deliberately singular in Hebrew — a collective noun that can mean one descendant or many. Paul will argue from this grammatical singularity (Galatians 3:16) that the promise is ultimately to "the seed" (Christ) through whom all descendants receive the inheritance. The Abrahamic land promise is not merely about real estate but about the eschatological inheritance.
16: "I will make your offspring like the dust of the earth" — the dust-comparison for the promised offspring is the third major image for the patriarchal multitude (stars in Genesis 15:5; sand in Genesis 22:17; dust here). Together these three comparisons (from above, below, and the boundary — stars/sky, dust/earth, sand/sea) represent total-encompassing uncountability. The dust image specifically echoes Genesis 2:7 (Adam formed from dust) and Genesis 3:19 (you will return to dust) — the human substance that seemed condemned to death (returning to dust) becomes the material of a promised multitude. What the Fall made into a death-image, the covenant promise makes into a hope-image.
17: "Go, walk through the length and breadth of the land, for I am giving it to you" — the walk-through command is a legal land-claim ritual. In ANE practice (attested in Hittite land grants and Mesopotamian property law), physical traversal of a territory was a recognized form of taking possession — the boundary-walk made the claim concrete. The same principle underlies the inheritance walk of Joshua 18:4,8 (Joshua sends men to survey the land before allotting it to the remaining tribes). Abram's commanded walk through Canaan is a symbolic land-taking performed under divine commission — the covenant-deed preceded by covenant-survey.
18: "The great trees of Mamre at Hebron, where he pitched his tents" — Hebron (approximately 30 km south of Jerusalem) becomes the primary patriarchal center of Genesis: Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah are all buried at the cave of Machpelah near Hebron (Genesis 23:19; Genesis 25:9-10; Genesis 49:29-32; Genesis 50:13). The "great trees of Mamre" (ʾēlônê Mamrê) mark a sacred grove — the site of the theophany of Genesis 18 where YHWH appears with two angels. Abram's altar here is his fourth in Canaan (Shechem, Genesis 12:7; Bethel, Genesis 12:8; Bethel again, Genesis 13:4; Hebron, Genesis 13:18) — a network of covenant worship sites that geographically stakes the covenant claim on the promised land through sanctified ground.
